purpose: Explains why the labor market has not deteriorated despite 525bp of monetary tightening, identifying the fiscal channel as the dominant force sustaining corporate profits and labor demand
components: Kalecki identity: Profits = Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends
Current fiscal deficit at 6-7% GDP provides ~$1.8-2.0T of profit support
Private GDP growth stripped of fiscal is only 1.0-1.5%
Financial conditions divergence from policy intent (equity rallies, credit compression) further supports labor
Immigration supply expansion complemented fiscal demand support
Corporate profits approximately equal investment + fiscal deficit - household savings - current account deficit
At 6-7% GDP deficit, fiscal channel sustains ~$1.4-1.6T annual corporate profits
Profit support suppresses defaults by estimated 100-150bp of spread compression
Self-referential instability extension: deficit creates Treasury supply that reprices term premium and eventually reverses the suppression
current_assessment: The fiscal buffer is real and currently the dominant credit cycle suppressant. No historical analogue exists for this deficit magnitude alongside a mature credit cycle. Whether the buffer merely delays or fundamentally alters the cycle is the central unresolved question.
limitations: The Kalecki identity is an accounting identity, not a causal model — causation is bidirectional
Fiscal sustainability concerns may eventually force consolidation
Does not specify which components of profit will adjust first under stress
Monetary-fiscal interaction effects are complex and regime-dependent
Cannot predict when fiscal impulse will contract
Whether buffer merely delays or fundamentally alters the cycle is unresolved
The 2pp GDP consolidation trigger threshold is contested
Self-referential instability loop has never been triggered by fiscal causes
